name: Vanuatu
_editorial_notes: Researched 2026-07-21 from official .gov.vu sources (ndmo.gov.vu, mocca.gov.vu, vmgd.gov.vu, police.gov.vu, moh.gov.vu). Not rendered by the app -- kept here for future maintainers.
Police emergency number is inconsistent even on the Vanuatu Police Force's own site: one page says '111 (Digicel) / 1111 (TVL)', a sitewide sidebar says just '1111'. Both are listed below.

No confirmed dedicated government ambulance number was found. 115 (Pro Medical, a private paramedic service) is the only number on an official .gov.vu page and is listed as such, not as a general government ambulance line. 112 appears on tourism/aggregator sites but not on any .gov.vu page -- deliberately not included.

Cyclone alert lead-times conflict between two NDMO pages (48/36/24 hours on the dedicated Cyclone Awareness page vs 24/12/imminent on the general Preparation for Disasters page). Used the dedicated Cyclone Awareness page's figures as primary since it's NDMO's specific cyclone resource.
NDMO's own earthquake guidance says 'Stop, Drop, Hold & Cover' (not the more internationally common 'Drop, Cover, Hold On') -- used NDMO's own wording since it's Vanuatu's official guidance.
A human phone verification of the numbers below (e.g. calling NDMO at +678 22699) is recommended before treating this as final.

cyclone.label: Cyclone
before: Blue Alert (cyclone forecast within 48 hours): stay tuned to radio, TV, SMS, or the internet. Secure and stow away loose material and rubbish, tie down your roof, and prepare cyclone shutters.
Yellow Alert (cyclone forecast within 36 hours): get ready and act now. Bring together the people you're responsible for, including bringing children back from school.
during: Red Alert (cyclone forecast within 24 hours): stay in a shelter or safe place. Turn off all gas and electricity and unplug all electrical items from the sockets.
Stay away from windows and doors. Do not go outside during a lull -- the calm center of a cyclone can be followed by winds from the opposite direction.
after: Wait for official confirmation it's safe before going outside -- downed power lines and debris are common right after a cyclone passes.
Follow updates from VMGD (vmgd.gov.vu) or NDMO for all-clear notices.

tsunami.label: Tsunami
before: Know the natural warning signs: a strong earthquake, a long earthquake (lasting more than 60 seconds), a loud roaring noise from the ocean, or the ocean visibly drawing back.
A tsunami can arrive within minutes -- do not wait for an official warning if you notice any of these signs.
during: If you feel a strong or long earthquake near the coast, move to higher ground immediately on foot. Don't wait for an official evacuation order.
If you can't reach high ground, get to an upper floor or the roof of a strong building.
Take an emergency kit with you if you can -- the all-clear can take more than 2 hours.
after: Wait for the official all-clear from NDMO before coming back down from higher ground.
A tsunami is a series of waves -- the first one might not be the biggest. Stay away from the coast until officially told it's safe.

earthquake.label: Earthquake
before: Know what to do in advance: Stop, Drop, Hold & Cover (Vanuatu's official guidance) -- stop what you're doing, drop down, get under solid cover, and hold on until the shaking stops.
during: Stop, Drop, Hold & Cover: get down low, protect your head and neck under something solid (a table, a doorframe), and hold on until the shaking stops.
Stay away from windows, mirrors, and anything that could fall.
after: Not every earthquake causes a tsunami, but if you felt a strong or long earthquake, move to higher ground immediately -- see the Tsunami section.
Expect aftershocks. Check yourself and others for injuries before checking on property.
